title: ESM import of `http` is slower compared to CommonJS
expected_behavior:
Importing a constant from `http` should not be significantly slower when using `import` vs `require`.

### What do you see instead?

Using `import` is a lot slower:

```
Running benchmark with 100 iterations...

Testing with http import...
.......... Done!
Testing without http import...
.......... Done!

============================================================
BENCHMARK RESULTS
============================================================

With HTTP import:
  Average:  26.88 ms
  Median:   26.08 ms
  Min:      25.19 ms
  Max:      67.84 ms

Without HTTP import:
  Average:  17.54 ms
  Median:   17.44 ms
  Min:      16.66 ms
  Max:      21.56 ms

Difference (with HTTP - without HTTP):
  Average:  9.33 ms (53.2%)
  Median:   8.64 ms (49.5%)

============================================================
```

### Additional information
observed_behavior:
Using `import` is a lot slower:

```
Running benchmark with 100 iterations...

Testing with http import...
.......... Done!
Testing without http import...
.......... Done!

============================================================
BENCHMARK RESULTS
============================================================

With HTTP import:
  Average:  26.88 ms
  Median:   26.08 ms
  Min:      25.19 ms
  Max:      67.84 ms

Without HTTP import:
  Average:  17.54 ms
  Median:   17.44 ms
  Min:      16.66 ms
  Max:      21.56 ms

Difference (with HTTP - without HTTP):
  Average:  9.33 ms (53.2%)
  Median:   8.64 ms (49.5%)

============================================================
```

### Additional information
maintainer_answer:
joyeecheung (MEMBER): I think it comes from the fact that at evaluation time, the ESM built-in facade needs access all properties of the built-in and set them on the namespace object. I think https://github.com/tc39/proposal-defer-import-eval might be able to help with this

joyeecheung (MEMBER): All built-in modules are treated like this - all properties are eagerly evaluated and populated at evaluation for ESM, compared to in CommonJS things are a lot more dynamic and lazy. Not sure how detailed this should be documented though, because it is more of an implication of the ESM semantics that everything is static v.s. in CommonJS, things are a lot more dynamic, and somewhat implied by e.g. https://nodejs.org/api/esm.html#built-in-modules and the existence of https://nodejs.org/api/module.html#modulesyncbuiltinesmexports - I guess a note can be added to remind users that built-in loaded from ESM is static and always eagerly populated in entirety.

joyeecheung (MEMBER): > but maybe also a call out to certain internal modules that do [heavy lifting](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/5af035503a1621dfac7b3d63e307c16671fc6c8a/lib/http.js#L118-L124) when imported like this

That might be a bit too much implementation detail to document and can easily get out of sync as people try to optimize it e.g. slicing undici up or something

resolution.fix_summary: Linked PR #59815: doc: highlight difference in module loading between ESM and CommonJS

linked_prs.title: doc: highlight difference in module loading between ESM and CommonJS
doc: add note explaining ESM vs CommonJS startup performance difference

comments.body: Oh, this was likely reported in https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/57649 already!

comments.author: joyeecheung
comments.body: I think it comes from the fact that at evaluation time, the ESM built-in facade needs access all properties of the built-in and set them on the namespace object. I think https://github.com/tc39/proposal-defer-import-eval might be able to help with this

comments.author: bpasero
comments.body: Yeah, deferred imports or just lazy import at the point of use helps us, but that makes one wonder what other core modules should be treated like this, would be great to get that documented somewhere.

comments.author: joyeecheung

Not sure how detailed this should be documented though, because it is more of an implication of the ESM semantics that everything is static v.s. in CommonJS, things are a lot more dynamic, and somewhat implied by e.g. https://nodejs.org/api/esm.html#built-in-modules and the existence of https://nodejs.org/api/module.html#modulesyncbuiltinesmexports - I guess a note can be added to remind users that built-in loaded from ESM is static and always eagerly populated in entirety.

comments.author: bpasero

> I guess a note can be added to remind users that built-in loaded from ESM is static and always eagerly populated in entirety.

That, but maybe also a call out to certain internal modules that do [heavy lifting](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/5af035503a1621dfac7b3d63e307c16671fc6c8a/lib/http.js#L118-L124) when imported like this, or even for common JS when certain properties are accessed.

comments.author: deepak1556
comments.body: > I think it comes from the fact that at evaluation time, the ESM built-in facade needs access all properties of the built-in and set them on the namespace object. Yup, [builtinStrategy](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/45a8b5503ec5f8f9ca924c9f784d0530bfc97cbd/lib/internal/modules/esm/translators.js#L413-L426) -> [getESMFacade](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/45a8b5503ec5f8f9ca924c9f784d0530bfc97cbd/lib/internal/bootstrap/realm.js#L355-L360) -> [syncExports](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/45a8b5503ec5f8f9ca924c9f784d0530bfc97cbd/lib/internal/bootstrap/realm.js#L368-L382)

comments.author: joyeecheung
comments.body:
> but maybe also a call out to certain internal modules that do [heavy lifting](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/5af035503a1621dfac7b3d63e307c16671fc6c8a/lib/http.js#L118-L124) when imported like this

That might be a bit too much implementation detail to document and can easily get out of sync as people try to optimize it e.g. slicing undici up or something

comments.author: axecalibur-dev
comments.body: I'd like to take up the issue.

comments.author: ayush-0513
comments.body:
Thanks for the explanation — that makes sense now! From what I saw in my benchmarks, the slowdown with import is just because ESM eagerly evaluates all properties in the built-in facades, while require stays lazy.

In practice:

**Servers don’t really feel it since the cost is one-time.**

Small scripts or CLIs can feel slower (~8–10 ms difference), so require or createRequire is still better there.

Tried a few workarounds (dynamic import(), caching, workers), they help but don’t fully remove the overhead.

So for now it looks like: use import for long-running apps, stick with require if startup time matters.

**I'd like to take up the issue**

comments.author: tysoncung
comments.body: Performance issues can be tricky. Have you profiled the code to identify bottlenecks? I'd be interested in helping optimize this if you can share some performance metrics or benchmarks.

Source: https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/60455
Final label: docs
GitHub labels: doc

Problem summary:
### Affected URL(s)

https://nodejs.org/api/stream.html#readabletoarrayoptions

### Description of the problem
Running the below code yields the error `ReferenceError: resolver is not defined`. 
```js
import { Readable } from 'node:stream';
import { Resolver } from 'node:dns/promises';

await Readable.from([1, 2, 3, 4]).toArray(); // [1, 2, 3, 4]

// Make dns queries concurrently using .map and collect
// the results into an array using toArray
const dnsResults = await Readable.from([
  'nodejs.org',
  'openjsf.org',
  'www.linuxfoundation.org',
]).map(async (domain) => {
  const { address } = await resolver.resolve4(domain, { ttl: true });
  return address;
}, { concurrency: 2 }).toArray();
```

I suggest instantiating the Resolver object first. For example:
```js
import { Readable } from 'node:stream';
import { Resolver } from 'node:dns/promises';

await Readable.from([1, 2, 3, 4]).toArray(); // [1, 2, 3, 4]

// added line
const resolver = new Resolver();

// Make dns queries concurrently using .map and collect
// the results into an array using toArray
const dnsResults = await Readable.from([
  'nodejs.org',
  'openjsf.org',
  'www.linuxfoundation.org',
]).map(async (domain) =>

text:
GitHub issue #43688: http.IncomingMessage aborted Event isn't the same as http.IncomingMessage close Event
Source: https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/43688
Final label: docs
GitHub labels: doc

Problem summary:
### Affected URL(s)

https://nodejs.org/api/http.html#class-httpincomingmessage

### Description of the problem

The "`aborted`" event is deprecated and states "`Deprecated. Listen for 'close' event instead.`" which isn't related in any way.
The `close` event fires whenever the stream is `CLOSED` while the `aborted` event only fires if the request is `ABORTED`.

For exmaple a little HTTP-Server:
```js
// server.js
const http = require('http')

const server = http.createServer((request, response) => {
    request.on('error', (error) => {
        console.error('> THERE WAS AN ERROR', error)
    })

    request.on('aborted', () => {
        console.log('> REQUEST GOT ABORTED')
    })

    request.on('close', () => {
        console.log('> REQUEST GOT CLOSED')
        response.end('CLOSED');
    })

    request.resume(); // CONSUME ALL DATA
});
server.on('listening', () => console.log('HTTP-Server is running.'));
server.listen(3100, '127.0.0.1');
```

And some simple curl requests to this http server:
`curl -F file=somesmallfile.bin http://127.0.0.1:3100`
```console
REQUEST GOT CLOSED
```

`curl -F file=somebigfile.bin --limit-rate 1K http://127.0.0.1:3100` and cancel ``CTRL+C``
```con

title: Inaccurate sentence about the event loop tick and the process.nextTick
problem_summary:
### Affected URL(s)

https://nodejs.org/en/learn/asynchronous-work/understanding-processnexttick

### Description of the problem

"_**Every time the event loop takes a full trip, we call it a tick.**_" - this sentence is very inaccurate. The "**_event loop full trip_**" phrase looks like if we are talking about one event loop iteration from "timers" phase to "close callbacks" phase.

So the next sentence "_**When we pass a function to process.nextTick(), we instruct the engine to invoke this function at the end of the current operation, before the next event loop tick starts**_" looks like the process.nextTick is invoked after "close callbacks" phase.
maintainer_answer:
joyeecheung (MEMBER): A more accurate description may be "Every time the runtime calls back into JavaScript for an event, we call it a tick".

There are still some nuances, the next tick queue checks are all over the place and in some APIs multiple checks can be done for one OS event at different internal processing phases just because the native land needs to call back into JS. At the same time this is not done consistently whenever the native land wants to call back into JS because of the overhead. So the "event" here really means Node.js events which may or may not correspond to OS events...

AugustinMauroy (MEMBER): Maybe transfert this issue to `nodejs/nodejs.org`

resolution.fix_summary: Linked PR #7204: content: clarify `process.nextTick()` timing

linked_prs.title: content: clarify `process.nextTick()` timing

title: [Docs] Wrong definition of assert.ifError(value)
problem_summary:
The definition provided for `assert.ifError(value)` [here](https://nodejs.org/docs/latest/api/assert.html#assertiferrorvalue) contains `value`, instead of `AssertionError`.

**Actual Definition**
```
Throws value if value is not undefined or null. This is useful when testing the error argument in callbacks. The stack trace contains all frames from the error passed to ifError() including the potential new frames for ifError() itself.
```
> The second word, should be `AssertionError`.

**Changes**
Changing [this](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/919ef7cae89ea9821db041cde892697cc8030b7c/lib/assert.js#L685) line in `lib/assert.js` would fix this issue.

title: --cpu-prof-name 'CPU.${pid}.cpuprofile' doesn't replace the placeholder
problem_summary:
```sh
node -p process.pid --cpu-prof --cpu-prof-name 'CPU.${pid}.cpuprofile' && ls CPU*
```

outputs
```sh
43689
'CPU.${pid}.cpuprofile'
```

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

always

### What is the expected behavior? Why is that the expected behavior?

Following the doc: https://nodejs.org/api/cli.html#--cpu-prof

> If --cpu-prof-name is not specified, the generated profile is named CPU.${yyyymmdd}.${hhmmss}.${pid}.${tid}.${seq}.cpuprofile.

I expect to be able to use those placeholder when I specify a name, especially when I use the `cluster` module to avoid having all the profiling overriding each others.

I expected the filename to be `CPU.43689.cpuprofile`

### What do you see instead?

```sh
'CPU.${pid}.cpuprofile'
```

### Additional information

_No response_
expected_behavior:
Following the doc: https://nodejs.org/api/cli.html#--cpu-prof

> If --cpu-prof-name is not specified, the generated profile is named CPU.${yyyymmdd}.${hhmmss}.${pid}.${tid}.${seq}.cpuprofile.

I expect to be able to use those placeholder when I specify a name, especially when I use the `cluster` module to avoid having all the profiling overriding each others.

I expected the filename to be `CPU.43689.cpuprofile`

### What do you see instead?

```sh
'CPU.${pid}.cpuprofile'
```

### Additional information

_No response_
observed_behavior:
```sh
'CPU.${pid}.cpuprofile'
```

### Additional information

_No response_
maintainer_answer:
joyeecheung (MEMBER): It looks like either a misunderstanding of what the doc says, or that the doc not being clear enough that the argument is not a pattern that will be filled by Node.js. The doc specifically says:

> If --cpu-prof-name is not specified, ...

which is not to be interpreted as "if it IS specified, Node.js will fill in the blank using a user-provided pattern". Rather, it means "if it IS specified, the name provided will be used as-is".

joyeecheung (MEMBER): labeling good first issue, I think it should be fixed by clarifying the docs that what's expected in the OP is not going to happen instead.

jasnell (MEMBER): I added the `feature request` label because it might actually be worthwhile exploring implementing this kind of replacement in the future. I agree that in the near term clarifying the docs is the best next step.

comments.body:
It looks like either a misunderstanding of what the doc says, or that the doc not being clear enough that the argument is not a pattern that will be filled by Node.js. The doc specifically says:

comments.author: joyeecheung
comments.body: labeling good first issue, I think it should be fixed by clarifying the docs that what's expected in the OP is not going to happen instead.

comments.author: BourgoisMickael
comments.body:
@joyeecheung could we have it as a feature to be able to use a pattern with placeholder in the name ?

Otherwise, using it with the cluster module, each process would overwrite the same file.

comments.author: cecia234
comments.body:
@BourgoisMickael I was writing the docs and trying out the parameter myself. Calling `node --cpu-prof --cpu-prof-name 'CPU.${pid}.cpuprofile'` I obtain the same output as you, but calling `node --cpu-prof --cpu-prof-name "CPU.${pid}.cpuprofile"` (using double quotes) actually outputs a file with the pid in the name. Could you try it? Please note that I'm on windows at the moment.

EDIT: after trying it on wsl, using double quotes I get a file with the name `CPU..cpuprofile`, so the behaviour could be OS dependent.

comments.author: BourgoisMickael
comments.body:
@cecia234 The `${}` syntax is a **parameter substitution/expansion** in shell (https://www.gnu.org/software/bash/manual/html_node/Shell-Parameter-Expansion.html).

Using double quotes or no quotes will trigger the substitution and replace it with the value of the variable `pid` or an empty string.

So on linux you end up sending `--cpu-prof-name CPU..cpuprofile` to the process. Hence the use of single quotes to send the literal string `${pid}` as argumen without shell substitution.

___

It doesn't seem like a big change to be able to handle pattern as name if it's already available when name is not provided

comments.author: cecia234
comments.body:
Oof, I didn't know that I'm sorry.
I agree with you that it does not seem like a big change, I'll keep the docs PR open until a more experienced contributor will give some feedback.

comments.author: jasnell
comments.body: I added the `feature request` label because it might actually be worthwhile exploring implementing this kind of replacement in the future. I agree that in the near term clarifying the docs is the best next step.

Following the doc: https://nodejs.org/api/cli.html#--cpu-prof

> If --cpu-prof-name is not specified, the generated profile is named CPU.${yyyymmdd}.${hhmmss}.${pid}.${tid}.${seq}.cpuprofile.

I expect to be able to use those placeholder when I specify a name, especially when I use the `cluster` module to avoid having all the profiling overriding each others.

I expected the filename to be `CPU.43689.cpuprofile`

### What do you see instead?

```sh
'CPU.${pid}.cpuprofile'
```

Maintainer answer / maintainer context:
joyeecheung (MEMBER): It looks like either a misunderstanding of what the doc says, or that the doc not being clear enough that the argument is not a pattern that will be filled by Node.js. The doc specifically says:

> If --cpu-prof-name is not specified, ...

which is not to be interpreted as "if it IS specified, Node.js will fill in the blank using a user-provided pattern". Rather, it means "if it IS specified, the name provided will be used as-is".

joyeecheung (MEMBER): labeling good first issue, I think it should be fixed by clarifying the docs that what's expected in the OP is not going to happen instead.

jasnell (MEMBER): I added the `feature request` label because it might actually be worthwhile exploring implementing this kind of replacement in the future. I agree that in the near term clarifying the docs is the best next step.

title: spawn doesn't use PATH when env is set
problem_summary:
Note: This issue only appears on linux. On windows the behavior is correct.

1) Place a binary in a non-default location `/test/hello`
2) Adjust your shell PATH env to include that location: `export PATH=/test:$PATH`
3) Run 
```js
let p = require('child_process')
proc = p.spawn('hello', [], {env: {test:'value'}})
proc.on('close', (code) => {
  console.log(`child process exited with code ${code}`);
});
```

According to the [documentation](https://nodejs.org/docs/latest-v12.x/api/child_process.html#child_process_child_process) this should work:
> The command lookup will be performed using options.env.PATH environment variable if passed in options object, otherwise process.env.PATH will be used.

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

All the time

### What is the expected behavior? Why is that the expected behavior?

`options.env.PATH` should only be used when it's actually provided by the user.

### What do you see instead?

spawn fails due to the binary not being found.
It uses the hardcoded fallback path, see https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/main/deps/uv/src/unix/process.c#L688
and https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/main/deps/uv/src/unix/process.c#L703

On windows however it always falls back to `process.env.PATH` if no `options.env.PATH` is set and therefore works as documented/expected, see https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/main/deps/uv/src/win/process.c#L974

### Additional information

I'm happy to create a MR for this

A bot will update them. Consider this example https://github.com/nodejs/node/pull/58124

Now that you opened a issue in libuv, I'll proceed to close this one.

Given that the issue is very likely not seen as a bug in libuv, should this issue be re-opened and a fix on nodejs side be implemented?
If not at least the documentation should be updated as it's clearly wrong

comments.author: arturgawlik
comments.body:
From what I see https://nodejs.org/docs/latest/api/child_process.html#child-process (**latest**) documentation properly describes current behaviour:

> The command lookup is performed using the options.env.PATH environment variable if env is in the options object. 

Above linked documentation (https://nodejs.org/docs/latest-v12.x/api/child_process.html#child_process_child_process) is for Node.js 12 😅

comments.author: bnoordhuis
comments.body: For posterity, it was amended in c649573a2ab. I thought that commit hadn't been back-ported to older release lines but it has (all that are still in support anyway.) Closing then.

title: Process documentation isn't sorted alphabetically
problem_summary:
### Affected URL(s)

https://nodejs.org/api/process.html

### Description of the problem

The Node.js documentation is, for the most part, sorted alphabetically (or, at least, within every conceptual section, it's sorted alphabetically). 

The `process` API documentation more-or-less follows this rule, with two methods being out of place - `availableMemory` and `execve`.

resolution.fix_summary: Linked PR #58403: doc: fix the order of the process API doc

title: Web Crypto doc page not responsive on mobile
problem_summary:
### Affected URL(s)

https://nodejs.org/docs/latest/api/webcrypto.html

### Description of the problem

Unlike other API doc pages, like [the WASI page](https://nodejs.org/docs/latest/api/wasi.html), the Web Crypto doc [page](https://nodejs.org/docs/latest/api/webcrypto.html) is not responsive on mobile. Self-explanatory.
Here are my failed attempts at fixing:
At first, I immediately thought it would be a problem inside the `<head>` tag. The page looked suspiciously zoomed out, like when it doesn't have `<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">`. Changing it wielded no results.
So I decided to actually get familiar with the build process for the API docs, and found out it injects media queries in a `<style>` tag. Tried manually changing the media queries of the affected page with one from a working page, didn't work.
I suppose I'm missing something in the build process that could be causing this problem. Tried investigating how the Markdown files are processed, but couldn't find much that could pinpoint the root cause.
maintainer_answer: jazelly (MEMBER): We have [nodejs.org](https://github.com/nodejs/nodejs.org) repo. This should be reported there. Closing.

comments.author: jazelly
comments.body: We have [nodejs.org](https://github.com/nodejs/nodejs.org) repo. This should be reported there. Closing.

So I decided to actually get familiar with the build process for the API docs, and found out it injects media queries in a `<style>` tag. Tried manually changing the media queries of the affected page with one from a working page, didn't work.
I suppose I'm missing something in the build process that could be causing this problem. Tried investigating how the Markdown files are processed, but couldn't find much that could pinpoint the root cause.

title: Why is AsyncLocalStorage named this way instead of something like Context or ContextObject in Node.js?
problem_summary:
### Affected URL(s)

https://nodejs.org/api/async_context.html

### Description of the problem

I'm curious about the naming of AsyncLocalStorage in Node.js. In Go, for example, there's a context standard library, which feels more intuitive when dealing with asynchronous context management. However, in Node.js, the API is named AsyncLocalStorage.

Why was this name chosen instead of something more straightforward like Context or ContextObject similar? Some developers find the current naming confusing or even misleading, as it doesn't immediately convey the idea of managing asynchronous execution context.

Is there any historical or technical reason behind this naming choice? Would it be possible to align with a more conventional term like Context or AsyncContext, which might be clearer for developers familiar with similar patterns in other languages?
maintainer_answer:
jasnell (MEMBER): The naming was chosen to align conceptually with the term "thread local storage" and it wouldn't be practical or helpful to the ecosystem as a whole to rename it at this point.

Qard (MEMBER): Also, changing the name to AsyncContext would conflict with the TC39 proposal of the same name. That even does _the same thing_ (roughly) so the API mismatch would be very bad for standards adherence.

joyeecheung (MEMBER): Closing because it is just a question and it’s answered.

comments.author: jasnell
comments.body: The naming was chosen to align conceptually with the term "thread local storage" and it wouldn't be practical or helpful to the ecosystem as a whole to rename it at this point.

title: Ambiguous wording at loading order description
problem_summary:
### Affected URL(s)

https://nodejs.org/api/modules.html#all-together

### Description of the problem

The following part at the linked page:
![1](https://github.com/nodejs/node/assets/66683424/598f919d-e71e-4cb3-994f-ae20790ad50e)

Can mean 2 things:
1. Sets `Y` as the file system root, so at step 3 `Y` means the same location as `./` .
2. Sets the file system root as a value of `Y` .

The 2.a. should be replaced with 1 of:
* `` set `Y` to the file system root ``
* `` set the file system root as a value of `Y` ``
* other

Source: https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/55340

Problem summary:
### Affected URL(s)

https://nodejs.org/api/util.html#utilabortedsignal-resource

### Description of the problem

The docs aren't entirely clear on the purpose of the second ~optional~ argument (`resource`), how it's supposed to be used, or when it's useful.  One of the primary use cases for this util function seems to be aborting a promise that doesn't support abort natively, but I'm not sure what I should pass as the resource?

```mjs
import { aborted } from 'node:util';

const ac = new AbortController();
const client = new SomeRandomDatabaseClient();

const results = await Promise.race([
  client.uncancelableQuery(),
  aborted(ac.signal, /* what am i supposed to pass here */),
]);

// somewhere else ac.abort() may or may not ever be called
// but assume ac does go out of scope and get gc'd eventually
```

Am I thinking about this use case correctly, or is not passing resource somehow creating a memory leak / preventing things from being cleaned up properly?

EDIT: It seems I misunderstood the second argument being optional, it appears required, but questions still remain how it's supposed to be used for the example above.

title: Are primordials safe from monkeypatching the [Symbol.species] method?
problem_summary: I already knew that certain JavaScript builtins can be affected by the value returned by the `@@species` method, but I was surprised to see how much it can muck with theur operation. Does anyone know if primordials are safe from manipulation of it? If not, is there a way to fix that?
maintainer_answer: avivkeller (MEMBER): CC @benjamingr

comments.author: avivkeller
comments.body: CC @benjamingr

comments.author: aduh95
comments.body:
> Does anyone know if primordials are safe from manipulation of it?

IMO this is not the question you should ask: primordials are just a tool to let internal code access the original value of the built-ins. Internal code that uses them is guarantee to behave as defined in the ECMAScript spec – i.e. if the ES spec defines a dependency on globally mutable things, primordials inherits this.

The more interesting question is "are Node.js internals affected by users mutating things?", and when the response is "No", primordials might be a solution – but it's not a silver bullet, they have limitations (which I tried to document in `primordials.md`) and using them have downsides (performance, code readability).

So, if you can report a feature of Node.js that's broken when the a `@@species` getter is modified, it would certainly be interesting to investigate, and the solution may or may not involve using more primordials. But it's a problem that would very much require a case-by-case solution, not a general thing.

> If not, is there a way to fix that?

Fixing it is not a goal – with maybe the exception of `Safe…` primordials, which purposefully deviate from the spec to be less reliant on globally mutable objects/methods.

title: Extending non-writeable by writing prototype properties fails
expected_behavior:
No error. I can't be sure what the spec says, but there's no error on this snippet in either firefox or chrome.

### What do you see instead?

```
TypeError: Cannot assign to read only property 'method' of object '#<Extended>'
```

### Additional information

_No response_
observed_behavior:
```
TypeError: Cannot assign to read only property 'method' of object '#<Extended>'
```

### Additional information

_No response_
maintainer_answer:
Renegade334 (MEMBER): This is entirely working as intended. You are probably evaluating in non-strict mode in your browser tests, where assigning to a non-writable property fails silently instead of throwing.

ljharb (MEMBER): This is referred to in TC39 as "the override mistake".  It's definitely language-level and not specific to any implementation.

comments.author: Renegade334
comments.body: This is entirely working as intended. You are probably evaluating in non-strict mode in your browser tests, where assigning to a non-writable property fails silently instead of throwing.

comments.author: conartist6
ljharb
comments.body: This is referred to in TC39 as "the override mistake".  It's definitely language-level and not specific to any implementation.

comments.author: conartist6
damianremington274-blip
comments.body:
Thank you for sharing that workaround. It is helpful to know that using
Object.defineProperty resolves the issue for your language runtime
implementation, especially given the "override mistake" constraints
mentioned earlier in the thread.

On Wed, Apr 8, 2026, 8:20 AM Conrad Buck ***@***.***> wrote:

> *conartist6* left a comment (nodejs/node#62604)
> <https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/62604#issuecomment-4206527778>
>
> I needed to do this as part of the implementation for a language runtime,
> so I was quite thankful to learn that there does exist a workaround. For my
> needs, it was enough.
>
> (function () {
>   "use strict"
>   class Base {
>     method() {}
>   }
>
>   Object.freeze(Base.prototype);
>
>   class Extended extends Base {
>     static {
>       Object.defineProperty(this.prototype, 'method', function method() {});
>     }
>   }})();
>

title: Error.isError does not detect legacy Error types using util.inherits
problem_summary:
Sample REPL output

> \> function CustomError() {}
> undefined
> \> require('util').inherits(CustomError, Error);
> undefined
> \> new CustomError() instanceof Error
> true
> \> Error.isError(CustomError)
> false

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

This happens 100% of the time

### What is the expected behavior? Why is that the expected behavior?

`Error.isError` is meant to be a more robust replacement for Error detection vs `instanceof`.  It should work consistently with legacy node patterns `utils.inherits`, without surprise.

### What do you see instead?

`Error.isError` does not detect error types that rely on `utils.inherits` for extension.  `instanceof Error` does still work in these scenarios.

### Additional information

The es-shim, https://github.com/es-shims/Error.isError/blob/main/implementation.js#L64 , has the `instanceof Error` behavior as the final fallback, which would catch this behavior.
expected_behavior:
`Error.isError` is meant to be a more robust replacement for Error detection vs `instanceof`.  It should work consistently with legacy node patterns `utils.inherits`, without surprise.

### What do you see instead?

`Error.isError` does not detect error types that rely on `utils.inherits` for extension.  `instanceof Error` does still work in these scenarios.

### Additional information

The es-shim, https://github.com/es-shims/Error.isError/blob/main/implementation.js#L64 , has the `instanceof Error` behavior as the final fallback, which would catch this behavior.
observed_behavior:
`Error.isError` does not detect error types that rely on `utils.inherits` for extension.  `instanceof Error` does still work in these scenarios.

### Additional information

The es-shim, https://github.com/es-shims/Error.isError/blob/main/implementation.js#L64 , has the `instanceof Error` behavior as the final fallback, which would catch this behavior.
maintainer_answer:
Renegade334 (MEMBER): [This is specified behaviour](https://sqlite.org/c3ref/interrupt.html):

> `Error.isError()` rejects values that aren't actual `Error` instances, even if they have `Error.prototype` in their prototype chain — `instanceof Error` would accept these as it does check the prototype chain.

This is exactly the same check that `util.types.isNativeError()` performs.

If you are using error objects constructed in that way, then you can't use `Error.isError()` to test for them.

ljharb (MEMBER): The es-shim package is best-effort, and it's unfortunate that it does "catch this behavior" - it shouldn't.

Renegade334 (MEMBER): Ultimately, if you are just trying to detect whether an object is derived from `Error.prototype`, you should be using `instanceof`.

`Error.isError()` is for realm-agnostic detection of objects constructed by the `Error` constructor, which is not the usage case you're describing. It's telling you that the objects you are passing it are not built-in Error objects, which is exactly its purpose.

This is exactly the same check that `util.types.isNativeError()` performs.

If you are using error objects constructed in that way, then you can't use `Error.isError()` to test for them.

comments.author: arciisine
comments.body: So the problem for me, is that this is from a third party library.  Knowing the construction method of an error is a high bar to put on library consumers.  This means every use of isError needs to be researched to determine is it's acceptable or every call site needs to have a logical or with an instanceof check.

`Error.isError()` is for realm-agnostic detection of objects constructed by the `Error` constructor, which is not the usage case you're describing. It's telling you that the objects you are passing it are not built-in Error objects, which is exactly its purpose.

comments.author: arciisine
comments.body: Appreciate the discussion, and understood on the goal for it to provide type checking across realms.  Hopefully this is a legacy issue that will only decrease in frequency, as I presume more and more people will reach for Error.isError by default, regardless of use case.

title: The behavior of FormData.append is quite strange
expected_behavior:
The expected output of file content will be the same.

### What do you see instead?

The content of the file has changed after being assigned a new name.

### Additional information

_No response_
observed_behavior:
The content of the file has changed after being assigned a new name.

### Additional information

_No response_
maintainer_answer:
Renegade334 (MEMBER): This is exactly the behaviour dictated by the spec (https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/form-control-infrastructure.html#create-an-entry).

If a new `filename` is provided, then a _new_ File object is created with the specified filename and the same byte data as the input. Because this new object is a File, not a MyFile, it doesn't have your custom methods.

If you want your custom File object to be passed through unaltered, you cannot specify a new filename.

If you want your custom File object to be passed through unaltered, you cannot specify a new filename.

title: https.ServerResponse pipe doesn't fully consume the response
problem_summary:
The following script will fail:

```js
const https = require('https')
const fs = require('fs')
const assert = require('assert')

const url = 'https://storage.googleapis.com/chrome-for-testing-public/145.0.7632.77/mac-arm64/chrome-mac-arm64.zip'

const request = https.request(url, response => {
  const contentLength = response.headers['content-length']
  response.pipe(fs.createWriteStream('test.zip'))
  response.on('end', () => {
    const stats = fs.statSync('test.zip')
    assert.equal(stats.size, contentLength)
  })
})

request.end()
```

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

100% of the time.

### What is the expected behavior? Why is that the expected behavior?

No error.

### What do you see instead?

```
AssertionError [ERR_ASSERTION]: 170216693 == '170231101'
    at IncomingMessage.<anonymous> (tmp.js:12:12)
    at IncomingMessage.emit (node:events:520:35)
    at endReadableNT (node:internal/streams/readable:1701:12)
    at process.processTicksAndRejections (node:internal/process/task_queues:89:21) {
  generatedMessage: true,
  code: 'ERR_ASSERTION',
  actual: 170216693,
  expected: '170231101',
  operator: '==',
  diff: 'simple'
}
```

### Additional information

_No response_
expected_behavior:
No error.

### What do you see instead?

```
AssertionError [ERR_ASSERTION]: 170216693 == '170231101'
    at IncomingMessage.<anonymous> (tmp.js:12:12)
    at IncomingMessage.emit (node:events:520:35)
    at endReadableNT (node:internal/streams/readable:1701:12)
    at process.processTicksAndRejections (node:internal/process/task_queues:89:21) {
  generatedMessage: true,
  code: 'ERR_ASSERTION',
  actual: 170216693,
  expected: '170231101',
  operator: '==',
  diff: 'simple'
}
```

### Additional information

_No response_
observed_behavior:
```
AssertionError [ERR_ASSERTION]: 170216693 == '170231101'
    at IncomingMessage.<anonymous> (tmp.js:12:12)
    at IncomingMessage.emit (node:events:520:35)
    at endReadableNT (node:internal/streams/readable:1701:12)
    at process.processTicksAndRejections (node:internal/process/task_queues:89:21) {
  generatedMessage: true,
  code: 'ERR_ASSERTION',
  actual: 170216693,
  expected: '170231101',
  operator: '==',
  diff: 'simple'
}
```

### Additional information

_No response_
maintainer_answer:
Renegade334 (MEMBER): The correct event to listen to here is the writable stream's `finish` event. As mentioned, the HTTP response emitting `end` does not imply that the pipeline is finished.

Renegade334 (MEMBER): Yes, the `end` event will indeed fire when all of the data have been read ("consumed") from the readable stream. That doesn't imply that streams further down the pipeline have finished processing those data.

comments.author: lukiano
comments.body:
When `end` is emitted, data may still need to be flushed to the file.

If you listen to `fs.createWriteStream('test.zip')`'s `finish` event, then it will succeed. The `pipeline()` utility of stream https://nodejs.org/api/stream.html#streampipelinestreams-options simplifies these operations.

comments.author: Renegade334
comments.body: The correct event to listen to here is the writable stream's `finish` event. As mentioned, the HTTP response emitting `end` does not imply that the pipeline is finished.

comments.author: richytong
comments.body:
> The 'end' event will not be emitted unless the data is completely consumed. This can be accomplished by switching the stream into flowing mode, or by calling [stream.read()](https://nodejs.org/api/stream.html#readablereadsize) repeatedly until all data has been consumed.

From [https://nodejs.org/api/stream.html#event-end](https://nodejs.org/api/stream.html#event-end)

comments.author: Renegade334
comments.body: Yes, the `end` event will indeed fire when all of the data have been read ("consumed") from the readable stream. That doesn't imply that streams further down the pipeline have finished processing those data.

comments.author: richytong
comments.body: I think "end" does imply that streams further down the pipeline have finished processing the data.

Maintainer answer / maintainer context:
Renegade334 (MEMBER): The correct event to listen to here is the writable stream's `finish` event. As mentioned, the HTTP response emitting `end` does not imply that the pipeline is finished.

Renegade334 (MEMBER): Yes, the `end` event will indeed fire when all of the data have been read ("consumed") from the readable stream. That doesn't imply that streams further down the pipeline have finished processing those data.

title: `ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND` when using `node` to run TypeScript that imports an ES Module with `.js` or no extension
problem_summary:
In terminal:
```bash
mkdir test
cd test
```
Create two files (copied from [TypeScript handbook](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/2/modules.html)):
```ts
// hello.ts
export default function helloWorld() {
  console.log("Hello, world!");
}
```
```ts
// main.ts
import helloWorld from "./hello.js";
helloWorld();
```
Then in terminal:
```bash
node main.ts
```

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

The bug reproduces if you import `./hello.js` or `./hello`, but not `./hello.ts`.

### What is the expected behavior? Why is that the expected behavior?

I expected the output to be
```
Hello, world!
```
even when `./hello.js` is imported, because the common practice seems to be to specify the `.js` file extension when importing an ES Module. See [MDN](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Guide/Modules#importing_features_into_your_script) and [TypeScript](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/2/modules.html#es-module-syntax) docs.

### What do you see instead?

```
minghinshi@Laptop:~/test$ node main.ts
node:internal/modules/esm/resolve:274
    throw new ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND(
          ^

Error [ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND]: Cannot find module '/home/minghinshi/test/hello.js' imported from /home/minghinshi/test/main.ts
    at finalizeResolution (node:internal/modules/esm/resolve:274:11)
    at moduleResolve (node:internal/modules/esm/resolve:864:10)
    at defaultResolve (node:internal/modules/esm/resolve:990:11)
    at #cachedDefaultResolve (node:internal/modules/esm/loader:718:20)
    at #resolveAndMaybeBlockOnLoaderThread (node:internal/modules/esm/loader:735:38)
    at ModuleLoader.resolveSync (node:internal/modules/esm/loader:764:52)
    at #resolve (node:internal/modules/esm/loader:700:17)
    at ModuleLoader.getOrCreateModuleJob (node:internal/modules/esm/loader:620:35)
    at ModuleJob.syncLink (node:internal/modules/esm/module_job:143:33)
    at ModuleJob.link (node:internal/modules/esm/module_job:228:17) {
  code: 'ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND',
  url: 'file:///home/minghinshi/test/hello.js'
}

Node.js v24.12.0
```

### Additional information

I first encountered the error using [ts-node](https://github.com/TypeStrong/ts-node) by calling something analogous to
```bash
npx ts-node main.ts
```
but later I reproduced the same error with just Node.
expected_behavior:
I expected the output to be
```
Hello, world!
```
even when `./hello.js` is imported, because the common practice seems to be to specify the `.js` file extension when importing an ES Module. See [MDN](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Guide/Modules#importing_features_into_your_script) and [TypeScript](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/2/modules.html#es-module-syntax) docs.

### What do you see instead?

    at moduleResolve (node:internal/modules/esm/resolve:864:10)

maintainer_answer:
Renegade334 (MEMBER): > Node.js ignores `tsconfig.json` files and therefore
features that depend on settings within `tsconfig.json`,
such as paths or converting newer JavaScript syntax to older standards, are
intentionally unsupported. To get full TypeScript support, see [Full TypeScript support](https://nodejs.org/docs/latest/api/typescript.html#full-typescript-support).

marco-ippolito (MEMBER): This behavior is documented and by design:
https://nodejs.org/docs/latest-v22.x/api/typescript.html#determining-module-system

comments.author: Renegade334

such as paths or converting newer JavaScript syntax to older standards, are

https://nodejs.org/docs/latest-v22.x/api/typescript.html#determining-module-system

title: Date is wrong
problem_summary:
Ciao!

How are you?
<!--
Thank you for reporting an issue.

This issue tracker is for bugs and issues found within Node.js core.
If you require more general support please file an issue on our help
repo. https://github.com/nodejs/help


Please fill in as much of the template below as you're able.

Version: output of `node -v`
Platform: output of `uname -a` (UNIX), or version and 32 or 64-bit (Windows)
Subsystem: if known, please specify affected core module name

If possible, please provide code that demonstrates the problem, keeping it as
simple and free of external dependencies as you are able.
-->

* **Version**: 9.1
* **Platform**: Linux
* **Subsystem**: Mint / Ubuntu / Debian

<!-- Enter your issue details below this comment. -->

Chrome:
```js
new Date()
```
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/28588124/32719937-4b71fc32-c862-11e7-8b47-2f83adf6ae8a.png)

The server:
```bash
date
```
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/28588124/32719960-5ea0f2cc-c862-11e7-92e1-a94b70e4cc43.png)

With node it shows wrong time (GMT):

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/28588124/32719988-7852919e-c862-11e7-8b76-6951b0455a8f.png)

It should show ```11``
maintainer_answer:
addaleax (MEMBER): It doesn’t look like there’s anything wrong here? 10:04:33.926Z is the same as 11:03:41 CET, it’s just displayed in a different timezone.

(If you need something that has better built-in support for timezones, you may want to use [moment-timezone](https://momentjs.com/timezone/) instead of the built-in `Date` class)

addaleax (MEMBER): > Why are they different?

They are the same time, they aren’t different, it’s just a question of how they are displayed

addaleax (MEMBER): `new Date().toISOString()` should give you the same result in browsers and Node, if that helps convince you

comments.author: addaleax
comments.body:
It doesn’t look like there’s anything wrong here? 10:04:33.926Z is the same as 11:03:41 CET, it’s just displayed in a different timezone.

(If you need something that has better built-in support for timezones, you may want to use [moment-timezone](https://momentjs.com/timezone/) instead of the built-in `Date` class)

comments.author: p3x-robot
comments.body:
Ciao! How are you?

I don't understand, in ```V8``` ```Chrome``` the ```new Date()``` is working, in ```NODE``` it is in ```GMT```.

Why are they different?

comments.author: p3x-robot
comments.body: It looks a bug.

comments.author: addaleax
comments.body:
> Why are they different?

They are the same time, they aren’t different, it’s just a question of how they are displayed

comments.author: p3x-robot
comments.body: Whatever. It is wrong.

comments.author: addaleax
comments.body: `new Date().toISOString()` should give you the same result in browsers and Node, if that helps convince you

comments.author: p3x-robot
comments.body:
That's a hack. ```Chrome``` works right, this is a bug, but whatever, I am using ```TZ="Europe/Budapest"``` before ```node``` and now it works.
It doesn't respect the timezone. It uses always ```GMT``` that's how it does it.

The ```JavaScript``` specification in ```new Date()``` respect the timezone.
What you are saying is a hack and whatever I closed its.

comments.author: p3x-robot
comments.body: I like to elaborate with other people as well.

comments.author: p3x-robot
comments.body: On the web, this issue is a problem.

comments.author: bnoordhuis
comments.body:
Today I learned GitHub has a 🙄 emoticon!  How useful!

> It doesn't respect the timezone. It uses always GMT that's how it does it.

In fact it uses UTC.  Closing this out again because it's emphatically not a bug.

title: Is Node.js v8.11.3 compatible with Windows 11?
problem_summary:
I have a legacy project running on Node.js v8.11.3, and I’m planning to upgrade the OS to Windows 11.

Are there any known compatibility issues when using this Node.js version on Windows 11?

I understand that v8 is officially end-of-life and not supported anymore, but I’d like to verify if anyone has successfully run it on Windows 11 or if there are known workarounds/issues.

Thanks in advance.

title: `console.log({ '"\'': 0 })` gives syntactically invalid JS
expected_behavior:
Either `{ '"\'': 0 }`, `{ "\"'": 0 }`, or `` { [`"'`]: 0 } ``

### What do you see instead?

`` { `"'`: 0 } ``

### Additional information

_No response_
observed_behavior:
`` { `"'`: 0 } ``

### Additional information

_No response_
maintainer_answer:
BridgeAR (MEMBER): This is intentional for readability reasons in the default case.

I do believe it would be valuable to have a option to generate JSON compatible output (a lot of things generate invalid output). It is just tricky to do that without slowing down the default case and it requires a substaintial change. Not slowing down the average case would for example be possible with a unique implementation, while that has the downside of otherwise not using the same feature set.

Would you be up to try to implement such option? I can't guarantee it would land though.

BridgeAR (MEMBER): I am going to close it since we have many inputs that would not be valid JS.

comments.author: BridgeAR
comments.body:
This is intentional for readability reasons in the default case.

I do believe it would be valuable to have a option to generate JSON compatible output (a lot of things generate invalid output). It is just tricky to do that without slowing down the default case and it requires a substaintial change. Not slowing down the average case would for example be possible with a unique implementation, while that has the downside of otherwise not using the same feature set.

Would you be up to try to implement such option? I can't guarantee it would land though.

comments.author: alexsch01
comments.body: It's also allowed in Chrome DevTools

comments.author: aduh95
comments.body: Why would you expect the output to be valid JS? That seems like a weird expectation, which is broken by many other inputs (e.g. `console.log({ fetch })` outputs `{ fetch: [Function: fetch] }`, which is also not valid JS). If you want something that outputs valid JS, why not use `JSON.stringify`?

comments.author: alexsch01
comments.body:
> > It's also allowed in Chrome DevTools
> 
> It's not, it throws the same syntax error as reported in the OP.

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8ebf551c-f0da-4497-a714-93b8fc04d751)

Google Chrome	133.0.6943.99 (Official Build) (64-bit) (cohort: Stable)

comments.author: aduh95
comments.body:
@alexsch01 `console.log({ '"\'': 0 })` is valid JS, no one is disputing that, it's not surprising it "works everywhere". The issue is about `({ `"'`: 0 })`, which is what Node.js outputs, and is not valid JS, hence works nowhere.
Anyway, it's just a misunderstanding, so I've hidden our comments to keep the issue focused on the original point.

comments.author: lionel-rowe
comments.body:
> Why would you expect the output to be valid JS? That seems like a weird expectation, which is broken by many other inputs (e.g. `console.log({ fetch })` outputs `{ fetch: [Function: fetch] }`, which is also not valid JS). If you want something that outputs valid JS, why not use `JSON.stringify`?

It's not a hard expectation, more a quality-of-life thing. I wouldn't expect it to hold for functions, instances of classes, etc., just things that can losslessly round-trip `JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(obj))` (plus maybe bigints), and even then only if the object is small-ish (i.e. doesn't need truncation). Further, I don't expect the output for such inputs to be valid JSON, only valid JS.

> Would you be up to try to implement such option? I can't guarantee it would land though.

Afraid not, I'm just raising this issue for completeness as I raised an equivalent issue against Deno and noticed Node suffers from the same problem. Feel free to close if this is considered out of scope.

comments.author: BridgeAR
comments.body: I am going to close it since we have many inputs that would not be valid JS.

text:
GitHub issue #57024: `console.log({ '"\'': 0 })` gives syntactically invalid JS
Source: https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/57024
Final label: question
GitHub labels: question, util, console

Problem summary:
```js
console.log({ '"\'': 0 })
// logs { `"'`: 0 }
```

However, `` { `"'`: 0 } `` is not syntactically valid in JS:

```js
let x
x = { `"'`: 0 }
// parse error: Unexpected token ```. Expected identifier, string literal, numeric literal or [ for the computed key at 2:7
x = { '"\'': 0 }
// ok
x = { [`"'`]: 0 }
// ok
```

While there's no hard requirement for `console.log` to always yield syntactically invalid JS (e.g. objects such as `File { name: "", size: 0, type: "" }`, truncation with `...`, etc.) I'd generally expect small JSON-compatible objects to be displayed in a way I can directly copy-paste into my code.

For comparison, Deno has [the same issue](https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/28101), while Bun doesn't.

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

Always, given an object with a key that contains both single `'` and double `"` quotes.

### What is the expected behavior? Why is that the expected behavior?

Either `{ '"\'': 0 }`, `{ "\"'": 0 }`, or `` { [`"'`]: 0 } ``

### What do you see instead?

`` { `"'`: 0 } ``

### Additional information

_No response_

Version:
23.3.0

### Platform

```text
Linux DESKTOP-3S4OV0F 5.15.167.4-microsoft-standard-WSL2 #1 SMP Tue Nov 5 00:21:55 UTC 2024 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux
```

_No response_

title: Why https module doesn't make a check for certificate revocation?
problem_summary:
Hello, didn't find any information and find it weird that https by default doesn't make a check for certificate revocation.
And in fact there's no normal way to do so using standard libraries. IMHO, this check is equally important as any other check, such as trusted CA or expiration date.

P.S. I'm testing on https://revoked.badssl.com, I'd expect that https (tls) module would throw an exception as it does for https://self-signed.badssl.com, for example.
maintainer_answer:
bnoordhuis (MEMBER): > And in fact there's no normal way to do so using standard libraries.

There is, look for the `.crl` option in the `tls` documentation.  You can also enable OCSP stapling.

bnoordhuis (MEMBER): CRLs and OCSP, plain and stapled, have enough practical concerns that turning them on by default is going to lead to widespread fallout.  A good overview: https://www.maikel.pro/blog/current-state-certificate-revocation-crls-ocsp/

Bundling CRLs with Node.js isn't useful because they would be out-of-date almost immediately (and before anyone asks, download-and-cache is not an option - too many failure modes.)

bnoordhuis (MEMBER): > about problems that I knew about before starting this thread...

If you knew about those problems, then why did you say it was "weird" it's not enabled by default?  Logic error, does not compute.

I'll go ahead and close this out, I think your question has been answered.

comments.author: bnoordhuis
comments.body:
> And in fact there's no normal way to do so using standard libraries.

There is, look for the `.crl` option in the `tls` documentation.  You can also enable OCSP stapling.

comments.author: astafev
comments.body:
as I understood from the documentataion, `crl` option accepts an already downloaded crl, which is impossible when we connect to a host that we do not know much about *before* connection. I'm talking about situation when we use https as a client libarary, so enabling OCSP Stapling won't work as well.

And, not less important IMO, I think that it should be enabled by default. Basic idea is that when I make an http request to some host, I expect that it's certificate would be checked. Now the situation is that some checks are performed (certificate path, expiration date), some are not.

comments.author: apapirovski
comments.body: I'm a little confused as to how you propose this would be done if not with a downloaded CRL? As far as I know that's literally what Chrome does, it updates it's CRL from all the different sources whenever it auto-updates itself.

comments.author: astafev
comments.body:
@apapirovski didn't look into Chrome implementation, if it does it this way, at least it should do updates every few hours. What I suggest is to make OCSP request (and CRL, not sure), if possible, enable it by default.

Just to clarify my point: open https://revoked.badssl.com/ in any browsers - it will show an error. `https` doesn't show any error/warning.

comments.author: apapirovski
comments.body:
This is just IMO but the default use case for the client side implementation isn't to query a bunch of random, unpredictable hosts. It would make no sense for the default to opt you into the whole mess of retrieving an updated list of revoked certificates and managing such a list. 

If part of your use case is dealing with random hosts and revoked certificates then it seems like that is something that you need to implement. Node does include the building blocks for users to implement this functionality which is IMHO enough.

To me this sort of mimics the discussion around CRLs within curl. https://bugs.debian.org/cgi-bin/bugreport.cgi?bug=745837

comments.author: apapirovski
comments.body: ping @nodejs/crypto

comments.author: bnoordhuis
comments.body:
CRLs and OCSP, plain and stapled, have enough practical concerns that turning them on by default is going to lead to widespread fallout.  A good overview: https://www.maikel.pro/blog/current-state-certificate-revocation-crls-ocsp/

Bundling CRLs with Node.js isn't useful because they would be out-of-date almost immediately (and before anyone asks, download-and-cache is not an option - too many failure modes.)

comments.author: astafev
apapirovski
comments.body:
@astafev There's a reason literally nobody out there offers this out of the box, including curl which is used for all sorts of things. Please read this article to understand the problem in more detail https://scotthelme.co.uk/revocation-is-broken/

If you have an elegant solution that even the browser vendors haven't been able to come up with then I'm sure node would consider a PR.

comments.author: sam-github
comments.body: Have you checked for an npm module that does CRL revocation checks? Using CRLs, if it makes sense in a particular environment, can be done in the callback to check cert identity.

title: Downgrade HTTP/2 to HTTP1 over HTTP(non-TLS)
maintainer_answer:
bnoordhuis (MEMBER): @chux0519 is exactly right. Downgrades work at the TLS level (ALPN is a TLS extension), not at the HTTP/2 level. `allowHTTP1` is only meaningful for secure servers.

mcollina (MEMBER): @kmannislands I would recommend you to open a fresh issue if you think this is worth to you. However I don't expect there will be volunteers to implement that exchange, but I think we'd welcome a PR if this matter ls to you.

resolution.fix_summary: Linked PR #34095: [node] Remove allowHTTP1 from http2.ServerOptions

linked_prs.title: [node] Remove allowHTTP1 from http2.ServerOptions

comments.author: chux0519
comments.body: I checked out the documentation; it seems the option `allowHTTP1` is for the method `http2.createSecureServer`, not `http2.createServer`. see [ALPN negotiation](https://nodejs.org/dist/latest-v10.x/docs/api/http2.html#http2_alpn_negotiation)

comments.author: bnoordhuis
comments.body: @chux0519 is exactly right. Downgrades work at the TLS level (ALPN is a TLS extension), not at the HTTP/2 level. `allowHTTP1` is only meaningful for secure servers.

comments.author: nwtgck
comments.body:
Thank you very much for your replies, @chux0519, @bnoordhuis!

I was wondering if it's possible to allow HTTP clients to use HTTP/1.1 in case of using `http2.createServer` in Node.js.

As an actual example, `nghttpx`, HTTP/2 reverse proxy in nghttp2 allows HTTP clients to use both HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 in the same port without TLS. This feature is very useful because it's highly compatible with conventional HTTP/1.1 clients.

comments.author: sam-github
comments.body:
Are you sure its useful? No major browser vendor has agreed to implement HTTP/2 without TLS, so even if nginx supports it, that doesn't mean there will ever be a client.

EDIT: Found the link. Uencrypted H2 is unused on the web, it is defined in the standards because IoT and other embedded vendors pointed out that requiring TLS on small devices is unreasonable, but no browsers are going to implement it.

See

- https://nodejs.org/dist/latest-v10.x/docs/api/http2.html#http2_server_side_example
- https://http2.github.io/faq/#does-http2-require-encryption

Given this, its not likely that node will go through a lot of effort to enable it, but if you are using node for IoT or something, and want it, I guess you could PR it.

comments.author: nwtgck
comments.body:
@sam-github Thank you for your comment!

> Are you sure its useful? No major browser vendor has agreed to implement HTTP/2 without TLS, so even if nginx supports it, that doesn't mean there will ever be a client.

I think Node.js has already supported HTTP/2 server without TLS.  As you see the example above, ``http2.connect(`http://localhost:${port}`)...`` was successful, which doesn't use TLS.

What I'd like to say was that I hope `http2.createServer({allowHTTP1: true})` to allow HTTP/1 connection as well as HTTP/2.

comments.author: sam-github
comments.body:
> I think Node.js has already supported HTTP/2 server without TLS

It does, but given that not a single browser supports that, the client has to be not a browser. Given the uncommon nature of that use-case, its not likely to happen anytime soon.

You could PR the feature, though, if its of interest to you. I don't think it would be declined.

You may also be able to implement it yourself, by sniffing the first bytes off the wire, and pushing the incoming stream to the right kind of server. test/parallel/test-tls-ticket.js has some code in it that might give you some ideas as to how.

comments.author: nwtgck
comments.body:
@sam-github Thank you very much.

> if its of interest to you

Yes. I'm very interested in the feature because **coexistence of HTTP/1 and HTTP/2 is more compatible with conventional HTTP connection** over non-TLS not only TLS.

> You could PR the feature, though, if its of interest to you. I don't think it would be declined.

I'm not familiar with a contribution to Node.js itself, I'm just a user of Node.js. So, I hope someone skilled people implemented the feature. But, for my learning, I'm reading the code of Node.js related to `http2`.

comments.author: patoi
kmannislands
comments.body:
Agree with @patoi, not enabling `allowHTTP1` on the non-TLS variant potentially pushes TLS into the application layer which is non-ideal when implementing a node.js server in something like an istio setup.

It seems that in the cleartext version, instead of ALPN which is indeed an extension of TLS, [http1.1 could be upgraded with an additional round trip](https://http2-explained.haxx.se/en/part5#id-5.4.-http2-for-http). While browser do not support h2c, clients like curl appear to support this. 

I think this should be re-opened as it is not as simple as the ticket initially appeared.

@mcollina @nwtgck @bnoordhuis would you accept a PR to add h2c upgrade support via the `allowHTTP1` flag for `http2.createServer`?

comments.author: kmannislands
comments.body:
See [http2 specification on this](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7540#section-3.2)

> A client that makes a request for an "http" URI without prior
   knowledge about support for HTTP/2 on the next hop uses the HTTP
   Upgrade mechanism ([Section 6.7 of [RFC7230]](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7230#section-6.7)).  The client does so by
   making an HTTP/1.1 request that includes an Upgrade header field with
   the "h2c" token.  Such an HTTP/1.1 request MUST include exactly one
   HTTP2-Settings ([Section 3.2.1](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7540#section-3.2.1)) header field.

For example testing with `curl` on a node.js h2c server, you can see curl following the spec but failing

```
curl -v --http2 http://localhost:12087/api/healthz
* Host localhost:12087 was resolved.
* IPv6: ::1
* IPv4: 127.0.0.1
*   Trying [::1]:12087...
* connect to ::1 port 12087 from ::1 port 50814 failed: Connection refused
*   Trying 127.0.0.1:12087...
* Connected to localhost (127.0.0.1) port 12087
> GET /api/healthz HTTP/1.1
> Host: localhost:12087
> User-Agent: curl/8.7.1
> Accept: */*
> Connection: Upgrade, HTTP2-Settings
> Upgrade: h2c
> HTTP2-Settings: AAMAAABkAAQAoAAAAAIAAAAA
>
* Request completely sent off
* Received HTTP/0.9 when not allowed
* Closing connection
curl: (1) Received HTTP/0.9 when not allowed
```

title: Can we supress the new experimental warning from the new default require(esm) setting?
problem_summary:
### What is the problem this feature will solve?

I use NVM for Windows.

1. I run `nvm install 23.0.0`
2. `v23.0.0` gets installed.
3. I then run `nvm use 23.0.0`
4. I then run `npm install npm@latest -g`

The latest npm gets installed, but I also get the following output pollution:

```
(node:32728) ExperimentalWarning: Support for loading ES Module in require() is an experimental feature and might change at any time
(Use `node --trace-warnings ...` to show where the warning was created)
```

5. If I then run `npm install svgo -g`, I get the same `ExperimentalWarning` pollution.

Is there any way I can suppress this message by default while still leaving the new default require(esm) setting enabled? I don't want to see this warning every time I install a global package, especially since I am automating things.

This doesn't happen with any node versions prior to 23.0.0.

Appreciate any consideration.


### What is the feature you are proposing to solve the problem?

A global configuration setting to suppress this new warning every time I install a global module in v23.0.0:

```
(node:32728) ExperimentalWarning: Support for loading ES Module in require() is an experimental feature
maintainer_answer:
targos (MEMBER): You can use [`node --no-deprecation`](https://nodejs.org/docs/latest/api/cli.html#--no-deprecation) or `export NODE_OPTIONS="--no-deprecation"`.

targos (MEMBER): Or disable `require('esm')` with `--no-experimental-require-module`

targos (MEMBER): I don't know much about Windows, sorry.

title: SSL Handshake Error using node >= 8.6.0
problem_summary:
<!--
Thank you for reporting an issue.

This issue tracker is for bugs and issues found within Node.js core.
If you require more general support please file an issue on our help
repo. https://github.com/nodejs/help


Please fill in as much of the template below as you're able.

Version: output of `node -v`
Platform: output of `uname -a` (UNIX), or version and 32 or 64-bit (Windows)
Subsystem: if known, please specify affected core module name

If possible, please provide code that demonstrates the problem, keeping it as
simple and free of external dependencies as you are able.
-->

* **Version**: 8.6.0 and 8.7.0 (works in 8.5.0)
* **Platform**: Windows 10 64 bit, MacOS High Sierra, all docker image variants
* **Subsystem**: https/crypto

<!-- Enter your issue details below this comment. -->

Starting with node 8.6.0, whenever I try to make an https call to an nginx server that I'm running (be it through a library like `request` or using `https.request()` directly), I always get the following error:

```
Error: 101057795:error:14077410:SSL routines:SSL23_GET_SERVER_HELLO:sslv3 alert handshake failure:openssl\ssl\s23_clnt.c:772
```

I'll admit up front that I'm not the most knowledge
maintainer_answer:
bnoordhuis (MEMBER): #15206 is indeed the PR that is responsible for this change.  I assume you don't pass an explicit `ecdhCurve` option to `https.request()`?

It sounds like #15206 slipped in an accidental bug fix because `ecdhCurve` is supposed to default to `tls.DEFAULT_ECDH_CURVE`, which is `prime256v1` at the time of writing.  `secp384r1` shouldn't have worked without a manual override.  Regrettably, we don't seem to have test coverage for that.

@nodejs/security What do we want to do?

bnoordhuis (MEMBER): > As a side note: I actually wasn't aware the ecdhCurve could be passed in to https.request().

It's not completely fool-proof at the moment.  If you make two requests to the same host using the same agent but with different `ecdhCurve` options, one or the other might be ignored.

If you'd like to work on that, the logic is in `https.Agent#getName()` in lib/https.js - it should include the option in the computed key.

Ideally, every option to `tls.connect()` would be picked up by `https.request()` automatically but I haven't been able to come up with something that is both robust and efficient.

bnoordhuis (MEMBER): > Looking at the options that are already there, I'm thinking it's reasonable to add support for the rest of the tls.createSecureContext() options since they all have serialization-friendly data types and should be reasonably short

@princjef Yes, sounds good.

> Nginx sets ssl_ecdh_curve to auto if it is not specified. How about we do the same?

@rogaps That's an option for v9.x, but not v8.x as that branch has been released, is stable and about to go LTS.  Related issue: #1495

resolution.fix_summary: Linked PR #16402: https: add extra options to Agent#getName()

linked_prs.title: https: add extra options to Agent#getName()
tls: set ecdhCurve default to 'auto'
fix `Append the ecdhCurve = 'auto' option to request options if node version higher than 8.5` (close #1401)
Add https option to prevent handshake error
Fix default ecdh curve for better compatibility
pass opts.ecdhCurve through to the https opts.ecdhCurve
oracle: new connector
Fix install in node ^8.6.0, ^9.0.0
Add rule no-dead-urls and progress spinner
Added workaround for SSL handshake error
improve the readme
fix: socketio realtime publish does not work with ssl_ecdh_curve value

comments.author: bnoordhuis
princjef
bnoordhuis
comments.body:
> As a side note: I actually wasn't aware the ecdhCurve could be passed in to https.request().

It's not completely fool-proof at the moment.  If you make two requests to the same host using the same agent but with different `ecdhCurve` options, one or the other might be ignored.

If you'd like to work on that, the logic is in `https.Agent#getName()` in lib/https.js - it should include the option in the computed key.

Ideally, every option to `tls.connect()` would be picked up by `https.request()` automatically but I haven't been able to come up with something that is both robust and efficient.

comments.author: rogaps
comments.body:
Hi all,
Nginx sets `ssl_ecdh_curve` to `auto` if it is not specified. How about we do the same? We set `tls.DEFAULT_ECDH_CURVE` to `auto`.

comments.author: princjef
comments.body:
@bnoordhuis Ah I see now. Yeah I'll work something up and submit a PR. Looking at the options that are already there, I'm thinking it's reasonable to add support for the rest of the [`tls.createSecureContext()` options](https://nodejs.org/api/tls.html#tls_tls_createsecurecontext_options) since they all have serialization-friendly data types and should be reasonably short (with the possible exception of `crl`, though I consider that on par with `pfx`, `key`, `cert` and `ca`, which are already included). Let me know if that sounds unreasonable.

As for supporting all options, I agree that supporting everything through something like `getName()` would be difficult to do in an efficient/robust way. Everything I can think of would require the signature of `https.request()` to change such that the user essentially indicates the desired mapping on their end.

comments.author: princjef
comments.body: @rogaps that sounds reasonable to me. I think the main question would be if there are any security concerns involved with being more permissive in the selection of the curve (I don't actually know if there are, just playing devil's advocate).

comments.author: bnoordhuis
Hativ
comments.body:
The default value of `ecdhCurve` should be `'auto'` or at least stronger curves should be added to the default value.

This breaks the `WebSocket` client from `ws` if the `https` server used for `WebSocket.Server` uses stronger `ecdhCurve`'s than the default value. See https://github.com/websockets/ws/issues/1227.

comments.author: bnoordhuis
comments.body:
I'll close this out because it looks like we're not going to roll back the change and as a question it's been answered.

@Hativ3 Can you open a new issue or pull request?

comments.author: ghost
comments.body: For anyone hitting this issue using https with the [request](https://www.npmjs.com/package/request) lib before https://github.com/nodejs/node/pull/16853 goes out, I was able to resolve by rolling back our node version to 8.5.x (`FROM node:8.5` for our Docker image)

* **Version**: 8.6.0 and 8.7.0 (works in 8.5.0)
* **Platform**: Windows 10 64 bit, MacOS High Sierra, all docker image variants
* **Subsystem**: https/crypto

<!-- Enter your issue details below this comment. -->

Starting with node 8.6.0, whenever I try to make an https call to an nginx server that I'm running (be it through a library like `request` or using `https.request()` directly), I always get the following error:

```
Error: 101057795:error:14077410:SSL routines:SSL23_GET_SERVER_HELLO:sslv3 alert handshake failure:openssl\ssl\s23_clnt.c:772
```

I'll admit up front that I'm not the most knowledge

Version:


Platform:


Subsystem:


Reproduction steps:


Expected behavior:


Observed behavior:


Additional information:


Maintainer answer / maintainer context:
bnoordhuis (MEMBER): #15206 is indeed the PR that is responsible for this change.  I assume you don't pass an explicit `ecdhCurve` option to `https.request()`?

It sounds like #15206 slipped in an accidental bug fix because `ecdhCurve` is supposed to default to `tls.DEFAULT_ECDH_CURVE`, which is `prime256v1` at the time of writing.  `secp384r1` shouldn't have worked without a manual override.  Regrettably, we don't seem to have test coverage for that.

@nodejs/security What do we want to do?

bnoordhuis (MEMBER): > As a side note: I actually wasn't aware the ecdhCurve could be passed in to https.request().

It's not completely fool-proof at the moment.  If you make two requests to the same host using the same agent but with different `ecdhCurve` options, one or the other might be ignored.

If you'd like to work on that, the logic is in `https.Agent#getName()` in lib/https.js - it should include the option in the computed key.

Ideally, every option to `tls.connect()` would be picked up by `https.request()` automatically but I haven't been able to come up with something that is both robust and efficient.

bnoordhuis (MEMBER): > Looking at the options that are already there, I'm thinking it's reasonable to add support for the rest of the tls.createSecureContext() options since they all have serialization-friendly data types and should be reasonably short

@princjef Yes, sounds good.

> Nginx sets ssl_ecdh_curve to auto if it is not specified. How about we do the same?

@rogaps That's an option for v9.x, but not v8.x as that branch has been released, is stable and about to go LTS.  Related issue: #1495

title: Missing stack traces from async functions after the first await
maintainer_answer:
richardlau (MEMBER): Cc @nodejs/diagnostics

hashseed (MEMBER): That's because Error.stack only reflects the actual stack. The inspector provides async stack traces, but Error.stack will never include the async parts due to the overhead of collecting it.

hashseed (MEMBER): You could run node with --inspect and enable async stack trace in DevTools.

resolution.fix_summary: Linked PR #72: TypeScript then babel

save stack trace to the client code in QueryBuilder and replace a stack if knex is configured with asyncStackTraces

WIP async stack traces ...

comments.author: richardlau
comments.body: Cc @nodejs/diagnostics

comments.author: hashseed
comments.body: That's because Error.stack only reflects the actual stack. The inspector provides async stack traces, but Error.stack will never include the async parts due to the overhead of collecting it.

comments.author: scottmas
comments.body:
Wouldn't it be nice if there was a way to enable async stack traces via a flag or something? Or when `NODE_ENV === 'development'` and the like? 

The overhead is huge, but there are cases when developers will want Error.stack to generate async errors for them.

comments.author: hashseed
comments.body: You could run node with --inspect and enable async stack trace in DevTools.

comments.author: scottmas
comments.body: I'm thinking something more command-liney would be better. Personally, I only use Chrome DevTools when I really want to figure out precisely what's happening. But most of the time, developers just want informative stack traces in their terminal.

comments.author: cjihrig
comments.body:
> something more command-liney would be better

Would `node -r longjohn` work for you?

comments.author: scottmas
comments.body: Arrr, meehaps

comments.author: hybrist
comments.body: Would it be possible to add a V8 option that enables async error stacks for "normal" error.stack properties? Or would that be too risky?

comments.author: gsathya
comments.body: We have [promisehooks](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1rda3yKGHimKIhg5YeoAmCOtyURgsbTH_qaYR79FELlk/edit#heading=h.sdpzjcyw06a5) in V8 that will help you build these async stack traces. Node can use this API to expose an async stack trace.

comments.author: AndreasMadsen
comments.body:
The @nodejs/diagnostics plan for solving this is:

1. upgrade to V8 5.7, this contains the PromiseHook C++ API – PR https://github.com/nodejs/node/pull/11752
2. Integrate PromiseHook C++ API to `async_hooks` and land `async_hooks` – PR: https://github.com/nodejs/node/pull/11883
3. This will allow module authors to create a long-stack-trace model that always works.

This problem is unlikely to be solved by node core directly, we will just give the tools to module authors such that they can solve it. For example I have written https://github.com/AndreasMadsen/trace, which uses an undocumented version of `async_hooks`. _This won't solve this issue as is, because we still don't have the PromiseHook C++ API, but it will eventually be updated._

Version: v7.7.3

The purpose of this issue is really a more broad request for better stack traces with async/await but I figured I would start with a very specific case. If we need to broaden the description, I'm good with that. I've seen a number of lengthy discussion on this subject in various places but I don't see any actual issues for it so I thought I'd start one and hopefully it's not just a duplicate of something I missed.

I'm filing the specific issue here because it seems the async/await functionality just added provides less useful error handling than we could get with generators.

```
async function functionOne() {
  await new Promise((resolve) => {
    setTimeout(() => { resolve(); }, 1);
  });
  throw new Error('Something Bad');
}

async function functionTwo() {
  await functionOne();
}

functionTwo()
  .catch((error) => {
    console.error(error);
  });
```
Outputs:
```
Error: Something Bad
    at functionOne (C:\Work\sandbox.js:5:9)
```

That stack is missing everything that called `functionOne` (`functionTwo` specifically).

The generator equivalent of this:

```
const co = require('co');

function* functionOne() {
  yield new Promise((resol
